epigraph.text:
Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will 
find; knock and the door will be opened to you.
epigraph.quote: The important thing is not to stop questioning.

content.text: {The important thing is not to stop questioning.}{Albert Einstein}

Factoring transforms a quadratic equation into a product equal to zero. 
A powerful principle—the Zero Product Property—then reveals the 
solutions.

content.title: Quadratic Equation
content.content:
A **quadratic equation** in one variable 
is an equation that can be written in the form:
\[
 ax^2 + bx + c = 0
\]
where $a$, $b$, and $c$ are constants and $a \neq 0$. This is called 
**standard form**.

content.title: Identifying quadratic equations
content.solution:
1. Yes (already in standard form, $a = 3$)

2. No (degree 3, not quadratic)

3. Yes ($2x^2 - 8 = 0$, $a = 2$)

4. Yes ($x^2 - 3x - 10 = 0$, $a = 1$)

content.text:
This property is the key to solving by factoring: if a product equals 
zero, at least one factor must be zero.

content.title: Applying Zero Product Property
content.solution:
By the Zero Product Property:
\[
 x - 3 = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x + 5 = 0
\]
\[
 x = 3 \quad \text{or} \quad x = -5
\]
The solutions are $x = 3$ and $x = -5$.

content.title: Solving Quadratic Equations by Factoring
content.content:
1. Write the equation in standard form ($= 0$).

2. Factor the quadratic expression.

3. Apply the Zero Product Property.

4. Solve each resulting linear equation.

5. Check solutions in the original equation.

content.title: Solving by factoring
content.solution:
The equation is in standard form.

**Factor:** $x^2 + 5x + 6 = (x + 2)(x + 3)$

**Apply Zero Product Property:**
\[
 x + 2 = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x + 3 = 0
\]
\[
 x = -2 \quad \text{or} \quad x = -3
\]

**Check:** $(-2)^2 + 5(-2) + 6 = 4 - 10 + 6 = 0$ 

$(-3)^2 + 5(-3) + 6 = 9 - 15 + 6 = 0$

content.title: Leading coefficient not 1
content.solution:
**Factor:** Using AC method: $ac = -6$, factors $1$ and $-6$ 
sum to $-5$.
\[
 2x^2 + x - 6x - 3 = x(2x + 1) - 3(2x + 1) = (2x + 1)(x - 3)
\]

**Solve:**
\[
 2x + 1 = 0 \implies x = -\frac{1}{2}
\]
\[
 x - 3 = 0 \implies x = 3
\]

content.title: GCF first
content.solution:
**Factor out GCF:** $3x(x - 4) = 0$

**Apply Zero Product Property:**
\[
 3x = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x - 4 = 0
\]
\[
 x = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x = 4
\]

Don't lose the solution $x = 0$!

content.title: Don't Divide by a Variable
content.content:
If you see $3x^2 = 12x$, don't divide both sides by $x$. You would 
lose the solution $x = 0$. Instead, move all terms to one side and 
factor.

marginNotes.text:
It is the glory of God to conceal a 
matter; to search out a matter is the glory of kings.

marginNotes.author: Évariste Galois
marginNotes.text: I have no time, I have no time!
